evidence_quotes.quote: customers need to be protected against digital publishers that want to destroy digital goods when they see fit.
that person must be the owner of that good and should be free to do whatever they want with their personal good.
they have decided to force online stores to de-list or even completely remove adult and mature digital content
they can decide what we can and cannot buy with our money
they need to be properly regulated and hold accountable by the law.

paragraphs.text: It is really great to see that this sort of initiative is taking place, and that the opinion of the citizens is taken into account. Democracy at it's best! I would like to point out that the customers need to be protected against digital publishers that want to destroy digital goods when they see fit. A great example of a civic movement is "Stop Killing Games", which I highly recommend you check out, since this movement goes beyond video games, and could ensure that any kind of digital provider of goods and services take into account the customers rights, because when somebody purchases a good, that person must be the owner of that good and should be free to do whatever they want with their personal good. This means that if I buy a digital product, I must be able to have it and use it as I see fit (in accordance with the law, of course) even if the publisher decides to discontinue the production of said good. We are the ones who decide when we will not use our property anymore, not someone else. And while we're on this topic, I would like to also point out the recent scandal that has developed, regarding the unchecked power that payment processors (Visa, Mastercard, PayPay, etc) have. Recently, due to pressure from an activist group outside of EU (Australia), named Collective Shout, the before mentioned payment processors have decided to force online stores to de-list or even completely remove adult and mature digital content, under the pretext that it was illegal, which it was not. It is incredible that an organization outside of EU borders can have such an impact inside of EU. This situation shows exactly what kind of power these people really have on the free market, since they can decide what we can and cannot buy with our money. Because their services and products are so essential to the free market and e-commerce, they have become an integral part of it, so much so, that none of us can opt out of. They are essential infrastructure at this point , and seeing what kind of power they can wield, they need to be properly regulated and hold accountable by the law. Just because some products or services can offend some people, this shouldn't mean that nobody should be able to enjoy them, as long as they are in accordance with the law. The beauty of living in a free society is that I can enjoy something, think in a certain way and behave like I see fit, without the fear that my personal beliefs and customs will lead to censorship. As long as I am not doing anything illegal, I should be able to enjoy myself, because we are free and your freedom ends where mine begins (and the other way around). To sum it up: 1) Customer protection against destruction of goods and services in the digital space by the publishers and forcing them to ensure a end of life strategy because buying means ownership 2) Customer protection against abuse of power in the digital space by payment processors who seek to censor and control what we can and cannot buy, and forcing them to abide the law and process our payments, not letting them decide what the law is or what it should be (we have governments for that) Thank you very much for your time and your attention!
